track_listing.details: LP-Never Turn Your Back On A Friend

transcript.text: On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. This is Thomas Vance here, and welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.

Tonight, we repeat our session by Angel Witch, and we broadcast a first-ever session by a band called More, who can hardly afford to gig, have no record contract or management, but they sound absolutely great.
And we got classic cuts by the sensational Alex Harvey Band, Blue Oyster Cult, Colosseum II, Budgie, and Yes.
We have new stuff from Peter Gabriel, Whitesnake, Tangerine Dream, and the Tigers of Pantang, among others.
And the return of the Friday Night Connection, that highlight of the week.
This evening we start with Frank Marino and Mahogany Rush.
[Music]
And Angel Witch from a session that we first put out during the month of February.
Good session as well, and we'll have more from them later.
Prior to that you heard something by Frank Marino and Mahogany Rush from their brand new album that's just come out.
The album is called What's Next, and the track we played was You Got Livin'.
And it's now nine and a half minutes after ten, and after this, there's something special.
And it's Peter Gabriel.
[Music]
[Music]
[Music]
Sensational Alex Harvey Band from an album called The Sensational Alex Harvey Band
in a series of albums called Rock Heavies.
One of my favorite tracks by them, it was called Faith Healer.
Let me tell you a little bit about the Rock Heavies series.
They're all on import from Germany, and it features loads of people like
Backman, Turner, Overdrive, Rod Stewart, The Runaways, Atlantis
Baker's Opera, Lucifer's Friend, Black Sabbath
Baker Govett's Army, featuring Ginger Baker and Van de Graaff, Generator
So they're called Rock Heavies and they're all available on import
And before that, something from the new and long-awaited album by Peter Gabriel
Which, just like the others, is called Peter Gabriel
The track was I Don't Remember
Got a cry from the heart here from a gentleman by the name of Paddy Hobbs
Who lives in Tonbridge in Kent
It seems that he's got a couple of O-levels to take
on the day that he has obtained his rush tickets for.
He's got three 450 rush tickets,
which are seats at the front of the circle,
for Wednesday, June the 4th at Hammersmith.
He wants to swap them for three tickets
of the same type of denomination and excellent seating
for Friday the 6th or Saturday the 7th or Sunday the 8th.
All those gigs, of course, are at the Hammersmith Odeon.
So if you can help in that particular cry from the heart,
why don't you write to us with your phone number
and I'll pass it on to Paddy Hobbs. Thank you.
It's 10.21, and now we turn to a bunch of people who literally are, if you like, out on a limb.
They have no management, no nothing, in fact, except a bit of talent.
It's five guys, Kenny Cox, Paul Todd, Brian Day, Frank Darch, and Paul Day,
and they call themselves More, and this is them, for the first time, on radio.
And that's a track from an album that I've had for about a week, and I don't take it off the turntable.
[Music]
It's the new album by Whitesnake. The album title is Ready and Willin',
and the track that we played there was called Sweet Talker,
and they tour throughout the month of June the UK.
And I played that specially for a gentleman by the name of Mark Phillips,
who knew he was going to be famous one day,
with lots and lots of best wishes from Dickie,
who lives at No. 4 Westwood Road, Allerton, in Liverpool.
10.29 on the Friday Rock Show,
and now Claire Kendall wants me to say hello to Brian,
whom she loves very much.
They live down in Bristol.
And Nick wants me to say, on behalf of all his mates,
that's Ben and Kevin, Doug and Enty Boy and the BCB,
would I please play a record coming up in a minute?
I've got to mention Brian Matthews
because he's after the same track as well
and Sean, I think it's
Weinstein of Highgate in London wants me to
play, well this, which is
Tangerine Dream
...a bunch of German musicians, that's just part of
Tangram Set 2, which is part
of their new album, or one side
of it, which is called Tangram
Tangerine Dream, for all the Tangerine Dream
fans who may be listening tonight
who've requested their new material, and indeed their old material
vociferously
I really hope that they tour again soon in this country.
I've never seen them, and I'm just dying to sit in the hall and get involved in that mood.
Back to Angel Witch.
That was E.T.I. off the album that came out in 1978 by Blue Oyster Cult.
[Music]
The track was called, or rather the album was called, Some Enchanted Evening.
And I played it specially for all culties everywhere,
and particularly for Nigel B., who's a mate of Phil Jones,
who lives at 15 Wyndham Street, somewhere in the Rhondda Valley.
and Jay of Ross-on-Wye in Heddafordshire
wants me to say hello to all his mates
that he used to go to school with at the Dovai School
I think it's called in Reading
especially Joe and Rabbit and Pig and Tobbs
and Mick the Red and Dobber
and also all the heavy metal freaks
who live in and around Ross-on-Wye
and before that you heard something by Angel Witch
from our session that we're repeating by them
that was recorded in February
the track was Extermination Day
and of course Angel Witch are going to go on a sort of mini tour
along with Crocus and Girls School
they'll be at the Lyceum this coming Sunday
Then they're in Newcastle and Birmingham and Manchester.
And just for the record, the other day I dropped into the studios of Maid of Ale
and saw a rather prostrate Mr. Wilson, who is a noted BBC producer,
raving around in between two speakers and recording crocus.
And it's a good session. That'll be coming up in the near future.
Also, we have recorded...
Well, we haven't recorded, but we're about to record Girl School,
who are very good, and we're going to be playing their single later.
Do you want some more?
Take me to music that came out in 1977.
It was Coliseum 2 from their album War Dance, and the track was called The Inquisition.
And that was specially for Charlie Watts and Andy Wainwright and any other jazz rock freaks around the country
who know a gentleman, well, I assume he is, by the name of Robert Terry of No. 41 Abbey Road in Marlborough in Leicester.
And prior to that, you heard something from our session by Moore, and the track was called Way of the World.
It's a minute now to 11, and it's time for the revival of the Friday Night Connection.
Didn't have one last week because we were doing our flashback.
But we're going to do it right now.
The tracks that we played for you were, four night ago that is,
Dolly Dagger by Hendrix, Hotel California, and that track by Stray,
the title of which completely eludes me,
as indeed as most everything else that happens in this world.
Nonetheless, the geezer that sent in the correct answer,
at least the one that we chose, was Mike Howell,
at number five, Blenheim Close, Wilmslow in Cheshire.
And the answer went like this.
dear tom this week's connection seems to be something to do with gruesome weapons like
daggers and guns and knives and the man was absolutely accurate mike you get yourself a
10 pound record token congratulations and i'm happy to say that you put together your own friday
night connection as well which is pretty clever but also is darn good music so if you think you
can find out what it is start listening about now 15 minutes after 11 o'clock and that was the
[Music]
friday night connection that was put together by mike howell who lives up there in wilmslow
in cheshire actually mike the more i look at it the more complex i think it is but if you
study the bits of music that we play there definitely is a friday night connection
or if you like a theme
or a thread running through
all three bits of music.
Think you know what it is?
Well, jot it down on a postcard
or any form of communication you like
and send it to me.
I'm Tommy Vance
at the Friday Night Connection,
the Friday Rock Show,
BBC Radio 1,
Broadcasting House,
London, W1A4WW.
And the first person
that we pull out of the sack
of enormous mail that we get
that we think has really got
the whole thing together properly
will get themselves a £10 record token, OK?
Don't forget, though, please,
to put together your own Friday night connection as well.
Back to our session from Moor.
[Music]
[Music]
And the track was called England is a Bitch.
A great bit of dub mixing there by Blackbeard, Dennis Bovell.
And before that, you heard something from our session by the first time that they've ever been on the radio.
The track we played was Soldier.
And I've got a communication here from Moore.
And it says, Tommy Vance, here's some more information.
Please read as printed.
It's psychologic, you know.
Moore, the Atomic Rock Band, are a five-piece band
who've been together in the present lineup for roughly eight months.
The band are Paul Cuppertee Day on vocals,
Kenny the Mad Axeman, God Cox on guitars,
Frank I Can't Help It Dutch on drums,
and Paul Tilly Todd on guitars,
and Brian Act Normal Day, no relation, on the bass.
Anybody who's seen the band live will know
that they are an exhilarating live act out to destroy
with their unique stage show, Catch Em If You Dare.
More have no real management, and they'd love a record contract.
Are you listening out there, record companies?
They'd like to thank all those people who've helped them,
including Peter Hinton and Saxon for their advice, good band, Saxon.
And, of course, Jimmy the Tyrant Norcott,
without whom all this would not have been possible.
Not to forget Debbie, the tyrant's wife.
Hello to Rita, Lorraine, Alan and Denny for the plug.
Watch the future. Heavy metal lives.
Last to mention, they're the finest heavy metal female band in the world
[Music]
Doing a session for us
Which will be broadcast in the near future
Before that you heard the Tigers of Pantang
And their new single which is called Rock and Roll Man
Little letter here from a gentleman
By the name of Andy Gouge
He lives at Pinewood Lodge
97, I think it's
Aldersford Road in Winchester in Hampshire.
Dear Tommy, he writes, I recently
attended the Deaf Leopard gig at the Gaumont
in Southampton. That was on Friday the 25th of April.
Now when I was there, I met a girl called
Bernadette. Four top
song, isn't it? I failed to ask her where she lived
but now I'm regretting it. I'd very
much like to get in contact with her
again for some good times, really.
I'm hoping that she might be a regular listener
to the programme so you might be able to help me. A brief
description of this Bernadette lady
from what I can remember is, she's about
Not five foot too tall.
She has medium-length fair hair with a parting in the middle.
On the night in question,
she was wearing a cream-coloured V-neck T-shirt and jeans.
I was standing with her near the front
throughout the Def Leppard act and their whole repertoire.
Yours faithfully, Andy Gouge, who's obviously heartsick.
If Bernadette is listening and you'd like to write to us
or maybe you even got his address in time,
well, just write to us, I guess, Bernadette,
and we'll send on your address
and all the other things that are necessary to be sent on
and the whole thing could become together.
Thank you. Sorry about jumping in on the end of that.
[Music]
[Music]
That was my mistake.
That was Angel, which, as I said, in Sweet Danger.
And before that, you heard Budgie.
And we were inspired to play Breadfan,
which, of course, came up on the album
Never Turn You Back in a Friend and then on The Best of Budgie.
Because this afternoon, we were going through a compilation
that's out on MCA, which is called Precious Metal.
And part of that compilation is the Budgie track Breadfan,
but it fades, all in that slow bit.
So we thought, no, we can't play that.
We'll play the original version,
which I think is one of the real classics.
and we played it also because we were castigated by Bruce Stocken
of 102 Gladstone Road, Broadstairs and Kent
who likes Budgie and he wants his name mentioned on the radio.
Bruce Stocken, you have been mentioned.
[Music]
Steve Howe and a Buggle reveal all tomorrow on Rock on Saturday.
That was Awaken, it's from Going for the One, it was by Yes.
I wouldn't worry about John Anderson with a voice like that,
he'll never ever be out of work.
That's a beautiful, beautiful track.
Before that we heard the last offering from Moore,
our session band of the evening who are doing their debut on radio
and incidentally they're going to be doing a couple of gigs in and around london they will
be at oscars wherever that is on the 27th of may which is next tuesday and at crackers which is in
wardour street that's on the 28th of may on wednesday and i do hope they get a break through
and via this broadcast that they did tonight because we really believe in them they're
marvelous musicians good entertainers and they really deserve to get a record contract
more we wish you well and now here's angel witch and that was angel witch an angel of death
And that's it for this edition of the Friday Rock Show.
Can I tell you about tomorrow's programme,
which is called Rock on Saturday between 4 and 7.30 in the afternoon?
We've got Brian Ferry, Peter Gabriel and Quartz
and a load of good music between 4 and 7.30.
I'd like to say a very special thank you to Sue Drum,
who is our programme secretary, who works so hard.
Our engineer tonight was Nigel.
Thank you, Nigel.
Next week on the Friday Rock Show,
the repeat of that amazing set put together by the Scorpions at Reading last year.
Not to be missed.
Speak, oh wise one.
I knew you'd enjoy more.
They're good, aren't they? They really are.
I hope some record company is having an earwig
and they give them a contract.
Deserve a contract.
I'm Tommy Vance in London,
and wherever you are,
we wish you the very best of luck, really.
Good night, and God bless.
